text: Hi, this is David Poynter, clinical instructor in gastrointestinal oncology at Moffitt Cancer Center. Today's case is a seventy-two year old male with an ampullary adenoma who had a history of endoscopic resection approximately eleven years ago. He had been surveilled at our institution for the past six years after the identification of recurrence and on most recent endoscopy was found to have a fifteen millimeter sessile polyp at the ampulla. Biopsy demonstrated tubulovilous adenoma with no high grade dysplasia. He is referred to surgical oncology as he was not felt to be amenable to endoscopic resection. Prior to operation, an ERCP with stent placement was requested in order to facilitate identification of the ampulla and lesion during an operation.

The patient is positioned supine in the split leg position with both arms tucked. The surgical team prepares for a robotic ampullectomy by placing four 8mm robotic ports in line above the umbilicus, along with a 12mm assistant port to accommodate the ultrasound transducer. The robot is positioned from the patient's right side.

The surgeon begins by dividing the gastrocolic ligament to mobilize the transverse colon inferiorly, separating attachments to the gallbladder and liver as needed. This maneuver exposes the duodenum, where the Kocher maneuver is initiated by dividing retroperitoneal attachments at the anti-mesenteric border. The robotic arm retracts the duodenum to facilitate exposure and division in the correct anatomical planes.

The surgeon uses intraoperative ultrasound to identify the ampulla, with a visible stent in the distal common bile duct. Stay sutures are placed on either side of the planned duodenotomy using V-lock suture, securing them to the abdominal wall for counter traction. Hook electrocautery is employed to create a duodenotomy, successfully identifying the ampulla and adenoma. A V-lock suture is placed through the superior aspect of the ampulla for retraction, delivering the ampulla and duodenum into the field. Hook electrocautery is then used to begin resection through normal duodenal and ampullary tissue.

The lumen of the bile duct is visualized, and the pancreatic duct is identified at the four to five o'clock position. The surgeon places the first V-lock suture to re-approximate the distal bile duct to the duodenum, ensuring the pancreatic duct is not occluded. A second V-lock suture is placed to approximate the superior bile duct to the duodenum. Circumferential resection of the ampulla and adenoma continues until the specimen is completely freed, then placed in an endocatch bag for pathology.

The surgeon uses the previously placed superior V-lock suture to approximate the distal bile duct to the duodenum in a running fashion, completing the circumferential anastomosis and tying it to the initially placed V-lock suture. The biliary stent is removed, and the vertical duodenotomy is closed horizontally using V-lock stay sutures, with a canal stitch closing the duodenotomy in a single layer from either corner.

Ultimately, these are tied in the center to complete the closure. The patient did quite well and was discharged on postoperative day five. Pathology demonstrated tubular adenoma with no high-grade dysplasia. Thank you for watching this video. Please like, comment, and share. Please be sure to subscribe to the channel to catch many of the upcoming content we have planned.
